diary:
I woke up at 7:20 with that clear early-June light pouring through the blinds, made coffee, and did my quick rooftop herb check like it actually matters to the whole universe. After a light breakfast at home in Newcastle, I ran through my calendar and tried to keep my head on the construction stuff—no drifting, even though it was sunny and the day felt like it had a plan.

I headed into Mercer Island and spent the morning at the Community & Events Center hammering out coordination notes and reviewing progress. Around lunch I grabbed something from Whole Foods and ate outside on the patio; the sun made even my spreadsheets feel less brutal. Later I swung by QFC for volunteer supplies, then spent the afternoon at Northwest Harvest sorting and packing donations. 

By evening, the light rail closure made me reroute downtown, and I ended up at The Triple Door for USA vs Wales. The place was buzzing early, and I left feeling genuinely tired in a good way.
thought_process: I tried to front-load the work because the day had moving parts—site coordination in Mercer Island, errands on Capitol Hill, and volunteering at Northwest Harvest. I chose the patio lunch because I knew I needed a real reset, not just food. The light rail closure (Westlake to University District) nudged my planning for getting downtown, so I kept an eye on timing and drove instead of relying on transit. The World Cup match being at 11am PDT also affected the vibe: I could feel everyone’s energy building, so I pushed to finish key documentation before the bars opened early. My mood started focused, softened with the sunny break, then steadied into warm satisfaction during volunteering. By match time I felt lighter—like the day’s effort finally clicked into something bigger.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
